items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Once again I’m tormented
by vague misgivings...
Shpak has a tape recorder,
the ambassador has a medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: The first clause is well rendered: slightly elevated/overwrought (“tormented by vague misgivings”) preserves the mock-heroic register that makes the line funny. The second clause, however, shifts the meaning: in context it’s not possession (“has”) but a parallel of losses/associated items (Shpak—tape recorder; ambassador—medallion), i.e., Bunsha is connecting two thefts. Using “has” weakens the detective-like inference and contradicts the situation (they no longer ‘have’ them). A better structure would be elliptical (“Shpak’s tape recorder, the ambassador’s medallion”) or “Shpak—his tape recorder; the ambassador—his medallion.”

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation:
Very nice, Tsar!
Very nice—hello, Tsar!

Tsar, very nice!
Hello, Tsar—very nice!
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: The translation preserves the core comic mechanism: repetitive, banal politeness clichés made absurd by constant “Tsar” address, matching Bunsha’s tipsy Soviet-formal social ritual. It’s not identical to the reference (which leans into self-introduction “I’m the Tsar”), but the effect is comparable and playable. Minor nuance loss: “очень приятно” is closer to “very pleased (to meet you)” than “very nice,” which can sound like evaluating the person rather than a greeting formula. Still comprehensible and funny.

items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Essentially identical in meaning, register, and punch. The imperious performative “I demand” is preserved; the line lands cleanly as a sudden power-drunk assertion. No meaningful loss.

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I’m in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Very close to the reference and keeps the archaic/high-register melancholy that clashes comically with the Soviet domestic setting. “I’m in sorrow” is slightly more modern than “I am in sorrow,” but still elevated enough for the gag.

items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Meaning is mostly preserved (being walled in), but the English is clipped in a way that loses the plaintive, bewildered rhythm of the original and the reference’s clearer subject-object relation (“They’ve walled me in”). “Bricked up” can also carry unintended slang associations in modern English, risking tonal derailment. Adding “They’ve” / “I’m” would keep the comedic lament and avoid ambiguity.

items.translation: See what the life-giving cross can do!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Good: keeps the religiously exalted phrasing (“life-giving cross”) and the triumphant attribution of a mechanical event to faith. Slightly less grand than “Behold the power…”, but still fits the Tsar’s voice and the joke in an atheist/Soviet context.

items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: The imperative captures the basic function (launching a mass dance), but it loses the announcer/MC flavor of the Russian catchphrase (“Everyone’s dancing!” as a proclamation) and the slightly bureaucratic/command-performance vibe that makes it funnier in context. “Everybody dance!” is serviceable but more generic and less quotable; “Everybody’s dancing!” or “All of you—dance!” would better preserve the performative announcement quality.

items.translation: Well, this is a lucky visit!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Captures the upbeat self-congratulation at stumbling into a profitable situation. “Lucky visit” is slightly stiffer/less colloquial than the breezy criminal charm of the original; “good call”/“timed that well” would sound more like Miloslavsky’s jaunty patter. Still works and keeps the comedic beat.

items.original: Икра черная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation:
Black caviar, red caviar...
and yes! Imported caviar—
eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Major creative miss: the reference explicitly avoids calling eggplant spread “caviar” to prevent confusion and to preserve the Soviet-status inversion joke (cheap eggplant spread presented as exotic delicacy). The actual translation repeats “caviar” and then “eggplant,” which can read as “eggplant caviar” (a real term in some cuisines) and blunts the satire. Also “imported” is less pointed than “overseas”/“foreign,” but the key issue is the ‘caviar’ label.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation:
Everything, everything I earned
by backbreaking labor,

is gone: three tape recorders,
three movie cameras,

three domestic cigarette cases,
three suede jackets.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: The list items are mostly correct and the escalating inventory gag is preserved. However, the punch in the original is the self-correction/afterthought: he says “куртка… замшевая… три” (a single jacket, then realizes he must also make it ‘three’), which exposes his exaggeration and is part of the humor. The translation smooths this into a clean, already-consistent “three suede jackets,” removing the stumble and the comic tell. Also, “domestic...” as a trailing qualifier is part of the rhythm; “three domestic cigarette cases” is fine but less performative than the broken phrasing. Net effect: joke weakened.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation:
And they’ll cure you!

And they’ll cure you too!

And they’ll cure me!
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Essentially identical in meaning and comedic resignation. The rhythm and escalating inclusiveness are preserved; punctuation/line breaks are subtitle-format driven and don’t harm the effect.

summary.interpretation: 2.27 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.45 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
